metadata.title: Seizn — Memory for AI NPCs
metadata.description: Plug into Inworld, Convai, or your own LLM. Seizn gives your NPCs persistent memory, relationships, and cross-generation recall — graph-priced, not per-seat.

hero.title: Your NPCs remember
hero.subtitle: Seizn is the memory layer for AI NPCs. Plug it into Inworld, Convai, or your own LLM — keep the dialogue engine you love, add persistence that survives sessions and generations.

features.title: Built for game studios
features.subtitle: Drop Seizn in, keep your dialogue engine. NPCs get memory, relationships, and persistence that survives sessions and generations.

smartExtraction.title: NPC / Faction / Event graph
smartExtraction.description: Model NPCs, factions, quests, and incidents as a graph. Relationships (trust, grudge, kinship) and events (witnessed, affected, actor) are first-class primitives, not schemas you have to invent.

instantRetrieval.title: Context-aware recall
instantRetrieval.description: Hybrid vector + graph retrieval returns only the memories relevant to this NPC, this scene. "Last time we met" queries in milliseconds.

enterpriseSecurity.title: Swarm-NPC scale
enterpriseSecurity.description: Per-entity metered pricing. Sims-like, Cities Skylines-like, and god-game titles run thousands of NPCs without per-seat cost walls. Self-host or managed.

codePreview.title: Integration in 10 lines
codePreview.subtitle: Keep your dialogue engine. Add Seizn for memory.

pricing.title: Per-entity pricing
pricing.subtitle: Start free. Scale on entities and events — not per seat. Works for 10-NPC indies and 10,000-NPC swarm titles alike.

enterprisePage.title: Contact Sales
enterprisePage.subtitle: Get a custom plan for your enterprise needs with dedicated support, SLA guarantees, and custom integrations.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.subtitle: Everything you need to know about Seizn

questions.q: What is AI Memory and how is it different from RAG?
questions.a: AI Memory is persistent storage that remembers user preferences, facts, and context across sessions. Unlike RAG which retrieves static documents, Seizn dynamically extracts and updates memories from conversations, creating a living knowledge base.
All data is stored on secure servers in the US (AWS) with end-to-end encryption. We comply with GDPR and CCPA. You can request complete data deletion at any time.
1) Sign up free, 2) Create API key, 3) Install SDK (npm install seizn), 4) Add memories with 3 lines of code.

questions.q: What happens if I exceed my plan limits?
questions.a: Monthly quota: We notify you at 80%. After exceeding, API calls continue at reduced priority. Rate limit (RPS): You'll receive a 429 error - use exponential backoff. Upgrade anytime for instant limit increase.
Yes. Delete individual memories, bulk delete by tags, or clear all. Deleted data is removed within 24 hours.
Any model: GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, Llama. SDKs for JS/TS, Python, REST API. Works with LangChain, LlamaIndex, Vercel AI.
Free plan is permanent: 12,000 memories, 1,000 API calls/month. No credit card needed.
Under 50ms average. Hybrid vector + graph search with optimized indexes.

whySeizn.title: Why Seizn vs Inworld / Convai alone?
whySeizn.subtitle: Dialogue engines do voices and personality brilliantly. None of them solve persistent memory at scale. Seizn plugs in as the memory layer.
whySeizn.tracingDesc: Use Inworld, Convai, NVIDIA ACE, or your own LLM for dialogue. Seizn layers memory on top — never replacing what works.
whySeizn.autopilotDesc: Graph storage, per-entity metered. Sims-like, Cities Skylines-like, and god-game titles run thousands of NPCs without per-seat cost walls.
whySeizn.governanceDesc: Grudges outlive characters. Faction history propagates to descendants. Kinship, witness, and relation primitives are built in — no custom schema needed.

pricingCta.title: Per-entity pricing
pricingCta.subtitle: Start free. No per-seat walls — 10 NPCs or 10,000, scale on entities and events, not engineer seats.

query.model: string - AI model: haiku or sonnet (default: haiku)

query.includeMemories: boolean - Include used memories in response (default: true)

faqPage.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faqPage.subtitle: Common questions about Seizn Memory API, from getting started to advanced operations.
faqPage.stillHaveQuestionsDesc: Check out our documentation or get in touch with our support team.

items.question: What is Seizn Memory and what problem does it solve?
items.answer: Seizn Memory is an AI memory infrastructure that provides persistent, searchable memory for AI applications. Unlike vector databases that only store/search vectors, Seizn includes the full product layer: memory extraction, policy management, key management, deletion, audit logs, and SDKs. It solves the problem of maintaining context across AI sessions and enabling personalized AI experiences.

items.question: How is Seizn different from vector databases like Pinecone or Weaviate?
items.answer: Vector databases are storage/search infrastructure for vectors. Seizn is a complete memory system built on top of that, providing: automatic memory extraction from conversations, memory type classification, namespace/scope management, API key rotation, audit logging, SDKs, and governance features. Think of it as 'memory infrastructure' vs 'search infrastructure'.

items.question: Do I need RAG to use Seizn?
items.answer: No. The most common starting point is: store memory -> search -> inject into prompt. RAG (automatic context composition + response generation) is the next step. You can start simple and add complexity as needed.

items.question: What's the fastest way to build a PoC?
items.answer: 1) POST /api/memories to store a user preference. 2) GET /api/memories to search. 3) Inject the results into your LLM prompt. 4) Later, add /api/extract for auto-extraction and /api/query for memory-augmented responses.

items.question: What should I store in /api/memories?
items.answer: Store information that remains valid across conversations: preferences (tone, language, format), facts (job, tools, project structure), instructions ("always summarize in tables"), relationships ("Alice is the team lead"). Avoid storing temporary or session-specific data unless using session scope.

items.question: What data should I NOT store?
items.answer: Never store: passwords, API keys, tokens, session cookies (auth info), SSN, passport numbers, bank accounts (PII), credit card info (payment data). For temporary data, use session scope with TTL if needed.

items.question: Why is namespace important?
items.answer: Namespace separates memories by project/tenant/environment. Without it, data gets mixed, search quality drops, and deletion/export becomes painful. Recommended: 'org:acme/app:chat/env:prod' or 'project:myapp/env:staging'. Never use just 'default' in production.

items.question: When should I use scope (user/session/agent)?
items.answer: user: Preferences that apply to a user globally. session: Goals or context valid only for this conversation. agent: Rules specific to one agent in a multi-agent system. Using scope properly reduces prompt length and improves response consistency.

items.question: What are memory_types and why do they matter?
items.answer: memory_type classifies memories: fact (unchanging info), preference (user choices), instruction (rules to follow), relationship (people/org connections), experience (past events). This is the most powerful axis for filtering, deletion, and policy application.

items.question: How do threshold and limit work?
items.answer: limit: Number of candidate memories to retrieve (too low = miss relevant ones, too high = noisy context). threshold: 0-1 similarity cutoff (higher = stricter). Start with limit=10, threshold=0.7. If missing memories, lower threshold to 0.6 and raise limit to 20. If getting irrelevant results, raise threshold to 0.75-0.8.

items.question: Why are my search results irrelevant?
items.answer: Usually one of: 1) namespaces are mixed, 2) too many memories stored (noise), 3) threshold too low, 4) content is too abstract ('likes things' vs specific facts). Fix: separate namespaces, make content specific, raise threshold.

items.question: Search quality dropped as memories grew. What do I do?
items.answer: Add importance scoring and keep only high-importance memories. Use TTL to auto-expire old memories. Periodically merge similar memories into summaries. Separate namespaces to reduce search scope.

items.question: How should I use /api/extract?
items.answer: Recommended flow: 1) Call with auto_store=false to preview extracted memories. 2) Show results to user for confirmation. 3) Store only approved memories. 4) Once extraction quality is proven, switch to auto_store=true for automation.

items.question: What's the difference between model=haiku and model=sonnet?
items.answer: haiku: Faster, cheaper, good for most cases. sonnet: More accurate, better for important extractions (onboarding, contracts, policies). Use haiku for bulk/initial extraction, sonnet for high-stakes scenarios.

items.question: Can I modify a memory after storing it?
items.answer: Yes, but the recommended pattern is: create new memory + delete/archive old one. This approach is better for audit trails and prevents regression issues.

items.question: How do I delete memories?
items.answer: Two approaches: 1) Delete by ID (precise), 2) Delete by namespace (bulk cleanup). For enterprise/compliance, ensure 'complete deletion + audit log'. Document your deletion policy clearly.

items.question: I'm getting 429 Too Many Requests. What should I do?
items.answer: You've hit the rate limit. Solutions: 1) Implement exponential backoff (1s -> 2s -> 4s). 2) Queue requests server-side. 3) Reduce request frequency: cache duplicate queries, batch operations, lower extract frequency.

items.question: How do I reduce costs?
items.answer: Biggest cost drivers: 1) Extract frequency - reduce calls. 2) Search scope - use namespace to narrow. 3) Model choice - use haiku for routine, sonnet for important. 4) Caching - cache repeated queries. 5) Batch operations when possible.

items.question: Can I use the API key in browser (frontend)?
items.answer: Not recommended - high risk of key exposure. Call Seizn from your server (Next.js Route Handler, Cloudflare Worker, serverless function) and have the browser call your server. Never expose API keys to client-side code.

items.question: What documentation does my security/legal team need?
items.answer: They'll want: 1) Data scope (what's stored/not stored), 2) Encryption (at rest: AES-256, in transit: TLS), 3) Tenant isolation method, 4) Deletion/retention policy, 5) Audit log access, 6) Key rotation/expiration policy. Keep a Security & Governance page in your docs.

items.question: What SDKs are available?
items.answer: We provide official SDKs for TypeScript/JavaScript (npm install seizn), Python (pip install seizn), and REST API for any language. All SDKs include TypeScript types, automatic retries, and streaming support.

items.question: What's the typical integration timeline?
items.answer: Basic integration (store/search memories) takes 1-2 hours. Adding extraction takes another 2-4 hours. Full production deployment with governance policies typically takes 1-2 weeks including testing and security review.

items.question: How do I handle multi-language content?
items.answer: Seizn's embeddings support 100+ languages. Store content in any language and search will work cross-lingually. For best results, keep a 'language' metadata field and filter by it when you want language-specific results.

items.question: Can I combine semantic search with keyword filtering?
items.answer: Yes. Use the 'filters' parameter to narrow by metadata (namespace, scope, memory_type, custom fields) before semantic ranking. This hybrid approach gives you both precision and recall.

items.question: How do I handle extraction failures?
items.answer: Check the 'status' field in the response. If extraction fails: 1) Verify input format is correct, 2) Check if content is too short (<50 chars often fails), 3) Try a different model. Set up retry logic with exponential backoff for transient errors.

items.question: Can I customize what gets extracted?
items.answer: Yes. Use the 'extraction_prompt' parameter to guide the model. You can specify: which memory types to extract, minimum importance threshold, specific fields to look for, and custom output schema.

items.question: How do I migrate from another memory/RAG system?
items.answer: Export your existing data to JSON with fields: content, metadata, created_at. Use our bulk import API (POST /api/memories/import) with batches of 100-500 items. Run a parallel validation query set to ensure quality matches.

items.question: What's the recommended backup strategy?
items.answer: Use the export API (GET /api/memories/export) to create periodic backups. Schedule daily exports for critical namespaces. Store exports in your own object storage (S3, GCS) with versioning enabled.

items.question: Is Seizn SOC 2 compliant?
items.answer: Seizn is built on SOC 2 Type II compliant infrastructure (Supabase). Our own SOC 2 certification is in progress (ETA Q3 2026). EU region deployment is available for Enterprise customers by request. Contact our sales team for security questionnaires or compliance documentation.

items.question: How do I implement user data deletion for GDPR/CCPA?
items.answer: Use DELETE /api/memories with user_id filter to remove all memories for a user. Enable audit logging to prove deletion. Set retention policies to auto-delete after a period. Export user data with GET /api/memories/export?user_id=xxx for data portability requests.

items.question: What is the Graph-RAG memory system?
items.answer: Seizn uses a Graph-RAG architecture that combines vector embeddings with knowledge graph relationships. Memories are stored as nodes with typed edges (relates_to, supports, contradicts, etc.). This enables: contextual recall through graph traversal, automatic community detection for topic clustering, and temporal knowledge tracking with validity periods.

items.question: What is the memory tier system (Hot/Warm/Cold)?
items.answer: Seizn automatically organizes memories into tiers based on usage: Hot tier (frequently accessed, kept in fast cache), Warm tier (occasionally accessed, standard retrieval), Cold tier (rarely used, archived but searchable). The tier manager automatically promotes/demotes memories based on access patterns, optimizing both performance and cost.

items.question: How does cross-lingual search work?
items.answer: Seizn stores both original content and a canonical English representation for each memory. Search queries are matched against both embeddings, enabling scenarios like searching in English and finding Hindi memories. We use multilingual embedding models (BGE-M3, LaBSE) that support 100+ languages with high cross-lingual alignment.

items.question: What languages are supported for memory extraction?
items.answer: Seizn supports 35+ languages for memory extraction and search, including: English, Chinese (Simplified/Traditional), Hindi, Spanish, French, German, Japanese, Korean, Russian, Ukrainian, Arabic, and all major Indic languages (Tamil, Telugu, Bengali, etc.). Language is auto-detected with 95%+ accuracy.

items.question: What is MCP integration and how do I use it?
items.answer: MCP (Model Context Protocol) allows AI assistants like Claude Desktop to directly access Seizn memories. Install our MCP server (npx @seizn/mcp-server), add it to your Claude Desktop config, and the assistant can automatically save and retrieve memories during conversations. This enables persistent personalization across sessions.

items.question: What technology powers Seizn's memory system?
items.answer: Seizn is built on: Next.js 16 with React Server Components, Supabase (PostgreSQL + pgvector for vector search), Voyage AI voyage-3 model (1024-dim embeddings), Claude for memory extraction and analysis. We use HNSW indexes for sub-100ms vector search, WebSocket realtime subscriptions, and Redis for caching.

items.question: How do I integrate Seizn with my LLM workflow?
items.answer: Three main approaches: 1) REST API - direct HTTP calls for full control, 2) SDKs (TypeScript/Python) - type-safe wrappers with retry logic, 3) MCP Server - automatic integration with Claude Desktop and other MCP-compatible assistants. For production, we recommend the SDK with server-side calls to protect your API key.

items.question: What is the Context API and how do I use it?
items.answer: The Context API (GET /api/context) returns a pre-formatted string ready to inject into your LLM prompt. It combines: user profile, recent memories, relevant facts, and graph context. Use format=brief (~500 tokens), detailed (~1500 tokens), or extended (~3000 tokens) based on your context budget.

items.question: What is the Seizn MCP Server and which editors does it support?
items.answer: The Seizn MCP Server (seizn-mcp) is a Model Context Protocol server that lets AI coding assistants directly access your Seizn memories. It supports Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, Cline, and any MCP-compatible client natively. For editors without MCP support (GitHub Copilot, Aider, OpenAI Codex), we provide config file sync that generates CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, .cursorrules, .windsurfrules, .github/copilot-instructions.md, .clinerules, and CONVENTIONS.md from your memories.

items.question: How does multi-editor config sync work?
items.answer: Seizn can export your memories as editor-specific configuration files. Use the sync_config_files MCP tool or POST /api/config-sync API. Supported formats: CLAUDE.md (Claude Code), AGENTS.md (OpenAI Codex), .cursor/rules (Cursor), .cursorrules (Cursor legacy), .windsurfrules (Windsurf), .github/copilot-instructions.md (GitHub Copilot), .clinerules (Cline), CONVENTIONS.md (Aider). Push syncs memories to files, pull imports files back to Seizn. This means your AI preferences follow you across every editor.

items.question: How do I authenticate the MCP server without copying API keys?
items.answer: Seizn supports OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628) for browser-based authentication. Run the auth_login MCP tool — it generates a human-readable code like 'ABCD-1234', opens your browser to seizn.com/device, and you approve access. The token is saved to ~/.seizn/credentials.json automatically. No more copying API keys between terminals.

items.question: What MCP Resources are available?
items.answer: Seizn MCP Server exposes read-only resources that AI assistants can access: seizn://memories/recent (last 10 memories), seizn://profile (user profile), seizn://graph/summary (knowledge graph stats), seizn://memories/project/{name} (project-filtered memories), seizn://context/{format} (pre-formatted context in brief/detailed/extended), and seizn://docs/setup/{editor} (editor-specific setup guides).

items.question: Can I get notified when memories change?
items.answer: Yes. Use the Webhook tools (create_webhook, list_webhooks, delete_webhook, webhook_deliveries) in MCP or the REST API at /api/webhooks. Subscribe to events like memory.created, memory.updated, memory.deleted and receive HTTPS POST notifications. This enables real-time sync between Seizn and your custom integrations.

items.question: How does auto context loading work with projects?
items.answer: When you call session_init with a cwd (current working directory), Seizn auto-detects your project from package.json, pyproject.toml, or Cargo.toml. It then loads project-specific memories and instructions automatically. This means your AI assistant always has the right context for the project you're working on — no manual configuration needed.

items.question: How do I set up Seizn MCP in my editor?
items.answer: For Claude Code: add to ~/.claude/settings.json under mcpServers with command 'npx' and args ['-y', 'seizn-mcp@latest']. For Cursor: go to Settings > MCP Servers and add the same config. For Windsurf: Settings > MCP. For Cline: MCP settings. Set SEIZN_API_KEY in the env block, or use auth_login for browser-based OAuth. Full setup guides are available at seizn.com/docs/integrations.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.subtitle: Common questions about Seizn Memory API, from getting started to advanced operations.

q1.question: What is Seizn Memory and what problem does it solve?
q1.answer: Seizn Memory is an AI memory infrastructure that provides persistent, searchable memory for AI applications. Unlike vector databases that only store/search vectors, Seizn includes the full product layer: memory extraction, policy management, key management, deletion, audit logs, and SDKs. It solves the problem of maintaining context across AI sessions and enabling personalized AI experiences.

q2.question: How is Seizn different from vector databases like Pinecone or Weaviate?
q2.answer: Vector databases are storage/search infrastructure for vectors. Seizn is a complete memory system built on top of that, providing: automatic memory extraction from conversations, memory type classification, namespace/scope management, API key rotation, audit logging, SDKs, and governance features. Think of it as 'memory infrastructure' vs 'search infrastructure'.

q3.question: Do I need RAG to use Seizn?
q3.answer: No. The most common starting point is: store memory → search → inject into prompt. RAG (automatic context composition + response generation) is the next step. You can start simple and add complexity as needed.

q4.question: What's the fastest way to build a PoC?
q4.answer: 1) POST /api/memories to store a user preference. 2) GET /api/memories to search. 3) Inject the results into your LLM prompt. 4) Later, add /api/extract for auto-extraction and /api/query for memory-augmented responses.

q1.question: What should I store in /api/memories?
q1.answer: Store information that remains valid across conversations: preferences (tone, language, format), facts (job, tools, project structure), instructions ("always summarize in tables"), relationships ("Alice is the team lead"). Avoid storing temporary or session-specific data unless using session scope.

q2.question: What data should I NOT store?
q2.answer: Never store: passwords, API keys, tokens, session cookies (auth info), SSN, passport numbers, bank accounts (PII), credit card info (payment data). For temporary data, use session scope with TTL if needed.

q3.question: Why is namespace important?
q3.answer: Namespace separates memories by project/tenant/environment. Without it, data gets mixed, search quality drops, and deletion/export becomes painful. Recommended: 'org:acme/app:chat/env:prod' or 'project:myapp/env:staging'. Never use just 'default' in production.

q4.question: When should I use scope (user/session/agent)?
q4.answer: user: Preferences that apply to a user globally. session: Goals or context valid only for this conversation. agent: Rules specific to one agent in a multi-agent system. Using scope properly reduces prompt length and improves response consistency.

q5.question: What are memory_types and why do they matter?
q5.answer: memory_type classifies memories: fact (unchanging info), preference (user choices), instruction (rules to follow), relationship (people/org connections), experience (past events). This is the most powerful axis for filtering, deletion, and policy application.

q1.question: How do threshold and limit work?
q1.answer: limit: Number of candidate memories to retrieve (too low = miss relevant ones, too high = noisy context). threshold: 0-1 similarity cutoff (higher = stricter). Start with limit=10, threshold=0.7. If missing memories, lower threshold to 0.6 and raise limit to 20. If getting irrelevant results, raise threshold to 0.75-0.8.

q2.question: Why are my search results irrelevant?
q2.answer: Usually one of: 1) namespaces are mixed, 2) too many memories stored (noise), 3) threshold too low, 4) content is too abstract ('likes things' vs specific facts). Fix: separate namespaces, make content specific, raise threshold.

q3.question: Search quality dropped as memories grew. What do I do?
q3.answer: Add importance scoring and keep only high-importance memories. Use TTL to auto-expire old memories. Periodically merge similar memories into summaries. Separate namespaces to reduce search scope.

q1.question: How should I use /api/extract?
q1.answer: Recommended flow: 1) Call with auto_store=false to preview extracted memories. 2) Show results to user for confirmation. 3) Store only approved memories. 4) Once extraction quality is proven, switch to auto_store=true for automation.

q2.question: What's the difference between model=haiku and model=sonnet?
q2.answer: haiku: Faster, cheaper, good for most cases. sonnet: More accurate, better for important extractions (onboarding, contracts, policies). Use haiku for bulk/initial extraction, sonnet for high-stakes scenarios.

q1.question: Can I modify a memory after storing it?
q1.answer: Yes, but the recommended pattern is: create new memory + delete/archive old one. This approach is better for audit trails and prevents regression issues.

q2.question: How do I delete memories?
q2.answer: Two approaches: 1) Delete by ID (precise), 2) Delete by namespace (bulk cleanup). For enterprise/compliance, ensure 'complete deletion + audit log'. Document your deletion policy clearly.

q3.question: I'm getting 429 Too Many Requests. What should I do?
q3.answer: You've hit the rate limit. Solutions: 1) Implement exponential backoff (1s → 2s → 4s). 2) Queue requests server-side. 3) Reduce request frequency: cache duplicate queries, batch operations, lower extract frequency.

q4.question: How do I reduce costs?
q4.answer: Biggest cost drivers: 1) Extract frequency - reduce calls. 2) Search scope - use namespace to narrow. 3) Model choice - use haiku for routine, sonnet for important. 4) Caching - cache repeated queries. 5) Batch operations when possible.

q1.question: Can I use the API key in browser (frontend)?
q1.answer: Not recommended - high risk of key exposure. Call Seizn from your server (Next.js Route Handler, Cloudflare Worker, serverless function) and have the browser call your server. Never expose API keys to client-side code.

q2.question: What documentation does my security/legal team need?
q2.answer: They'll want: 1) Data scope (what's stored/not stored), 2) Encryption (at rest: AES-256, in transit: TLS), 3) Tenant isolation method, 4) Deletion/retention policy, 5) Audit log access, 6) Key rotation/expiration policy. Keep a Security & Governance page in your docs.

consent.metadataDescription: Draft consent management form for subject-scoped memory storage and AI training scopes.
consent.title: Subject consent
consent.subtitle: Manage the subject-level permissions Seizn checks before retaining regulated memory.

scopes.label: Memory storage
scopes.description: Store subject-keyed memory for in-game personalization.

scopes.label: AI training
scopes.description: Use subject data to improve models, evaluators, or retrieval behavior.

scopes.label: Analytics
scopes.description: Use subject events in aggregate product and quality metrics.

scopes.label: Persona seeding (Korean studios)
scopes.description: Seed synthetic NPC people from Nemotron-Personas-Korea into this studio workspace.

sla.metadataDescription: Public SLA for Seizn Pro and Enterprise customers.
sla.title: Seizn Service Level Agreement
sla.subtitle: The public availability commitment for teams running Seizn Pro or Enterprise in production.
sla.scopeBody: This SLA applies to Pro ($999/mo) and Enterprise plans only. Free, Indie, and Studio plans are not covered by service credits.
sla.uptimeBody: Seizn commits to 99.9% monthly availability, excluding scheduled maintenance announced at least 72 hours in advance.
sla.measurementBody: Availability is measured on api.seizn.com/v1/* endpoints by an external ping every 60 seconds.
exclusions: Customer misconfiguration or client-side integration errors.
Third-party dependency outages, including Stripe and upstream Supabase incidents.
Scheduled maintenance announced at least 72 hours in advance.
sla.claimBody: Email within 30 days of the incident with the affected workspace, time window, and impact summary:
sla.incidentsBody: Public incident history and current availability are published on the Seizn status page:

hero.title: Catch every
hero.subtitle: Type a fact. Seizn reconciles it against your canon: 8 characters, 22 rules, and a 30 day timeline. Your review is the verdict.

workflow.title: Three steps. One ledger.
workflow.subtitle: Import, review, and write with the same canon behind every keystroke.

steps.title: Import
steps.subtitle: Bring your canon in.
steps.body: Drop a manuscript, point at a Google Doc, or sync a worldbuilding file. Seizn parses characters, locations, and rules into a structured ledger.

steps.title: Review
steps.subtitle: Your verdict, recorded.
steps.body: Every fact lands with a verdict: accepted, queued, or in conflict. Your review becomes the source of truth, replayable on demand.

steps.title: Write
steps.subtitle: Draft inside the ledger.
steps.body: Compose new scenes alongside the graph. Contradictions surface the moment they appear, with the rule cited and one click to override.

inputs.title: Meet your work where it lives.
inputs.subtitle: Four ingest modes. Same ledger, same review, same canon.

modes.name: Native editor
modes.subtitle: Author inside Seizn.
modes.body: Write directly in the canon-aware editor. Every paragraph is checked against the ledger as you type.

modes.name: DOCX import
modes.subtitle: Bring a manuscript.
modes.body: Drag in a .docx, .rtf, or .odt file. Seizn extracts characters, scenes, and dialogue into the ledger.

modes.name: Plain text
modes.subtitle: Markdown-friendly.
modes.body: Paste or upload .md and .txt. Headings become scenes; mentions become entities. Round-trip is lossless.

modes.name: Google Docs
modes.subtitle: Sync, do not copy.
modes.body: Connect a Doc once. Seizn keeps the ledger in sync and surfaces conflicts as comment threads.

conflicts.title: Every contradiction, cited and ranked.
conflicts.subtitle: Severity is signal, not noise. Critical breaks canon outright. Minor flags a soft drift you may want to keep.
conflicts.graphNote: The graph is not cosmetic. Every conflict points to the exact node and edge that broke.

items.fact: Chapter 11: Han Iseul has gray eyes.
Chapter 7: Park Jio enters the rooftop on day 9.
items.against: Rule r03 locks rooftop access until day 14.

items.fact: Chapter 18: Yun Hana attends astronomy club.
items.against: Canon says photo club. It may be a transfer event.

simulation.title: Replay before you commit.
simulation.subtitle: Stage a scene against the ledger. Seizn projects downstream impact at the token level: safe paths and risk paths, ranked.

candidates.label: Safe path
candidates.title: Han Iseul stays at the observatory until dawn.
candidates.body: Honors r02 and r04. No new conflicts. Continuity stays intact.

candidates.label: Risk path
candidates.title: Han Iseul leaves before the eclipse peak.
candidates.body: Drops r02 anchoring. Two downstream scenes lose their grounding event.

trust.title: Author-grade by default.
trust.subtitle: Decisions you can defend, infrastructure you can audit, separation rules you can prove.

items.title: Workspace-isolated
items.body: Sample IP is synthetic. Your manuscript never trains a public model.

items.title: BYOK supported
items.body: Bring your own Anthropic key. 50% off list price, unlimited tokens.

items.title: Replayable canon
items.body: Every verdict is logged. Roll back, branch, or audit any decision.

items.title: SFW-only policy
items.body: Litheon LLC operates Seizn under a strict safe-for-work content rule.

pricing.title: Pay for the ledger, not the LLM.
pricing.subtitle: BYOK halves the price and lifts the token cap. Yearly saves 15%. 30-day trial on every tier.

blurbs.indie: For solo authors holding their own canon.
blurbs.pro: For pro authors and small studios shipping multiple IPs.
blurbs.studio: Multi-IP, multi-author. Audit log, role permissions, dedicated review queue.
blurbs.enterprise: Custom data residency, SSO, on-prem replay archive, premium SLA.

items.q: How is Seizn different from Sudowrite or NovelCrafter?
items.a: Seizn is not a writing assistant. It is a canon ledger. We do not generate your prose; we hold your facts to the letter and surface contradictions as they appear.
Bring your own Anthropic key and pay 50% of list price with unlimited tokens.
Always. Every ledger exports as structured JSON, plain markdown, or DOCX. No lock-in. Cancel anytime and leave with your work.
Your work is isolated from sample IPs and from any public model training. The Saebyeok demo on this page is synthetic data, not a real customer.

items.q: Does the trial need a credit card?
items.a: No. 30 days, no card. After day 30, pick a tier or your project archives in read-only mode for another 60 days.

footer.tagline: Author memory, held to the letter. SFW-only. Built for canon authority.
footer.version: v1.0 · saebyeok demo is synthetic data

engine.body: Building game NPC AI? The same canon engine, exposed as an SDK.
